1: If then you have been raised with Christ, seek the things that are above (ta anō), where Christ is, seated at the right hand of God — the upward orientation (ta anō — the above things) responds to the Colossian philosophy's interest in heavenly realities. Rather than spiritual ascent through mystical practice and angelic veneration, Paul reorients the upward gaze toward the risen and enthroned Christ.
2: Set your minds on things that are above, not on things that are on earth — the contrast between the heavenly (epouranios) and the earthly (epigeios) reflects standard Platonic dualism — but Paul transforms it: the heavenly is not an abstract realm of ideas but the specific place where the risen Christ is enthroned. The Platonic framework is adopted and christologically redefined.
3: For you have died, and your life is hidden with Christ in God — the hiddenness (kekryptai — perfect passive: is and remains hidden) of the believer's life in the divine reality corresponds to the apocalyptic concept of the hidden things that will be revealed at the end. The believer's true identity is eschatologically concealed until the parousia.
4: When Christ who is your life appears, then you also will appear with him in glory — the parousia (appearing, coming) as the moment of eschatological disclosure. The glory (doxa) is the Shekinah-glory that characterized YHWH's presence in the OT and that will characterize the final revelation of the redeemed. Christ's appearing is simultaneously the community's appearing.
5: Put to death therefore what is earthly in you: sexual immorality, impurity, passion, evil desire, and covetousness, which is idolatry — the vice list (porneia, akatharsia, pathos, epithymia kakē, pleonexia) is a standard form in Hellenistic Jewish moral teaching (the Two Ways literature, the Testaments of the Twelve Patriarchs) adapted to describe the Gentile condition. Covetousness as idolatry appears in Ephesians 5:5 and reflects the Jewish understanding that all sin is ultimately the worship of something other than God.
6: On account of these the wrath of God is coming — the divine wrath (orgē theou) as the eschatological consequence of idolatry reflects both OT prophetic tradition (Isaiah 13:11; Jeremiah 25:15-16) and Jewish apocalyptic (1 Enoch Colossians 91:9; Romans 1:18). The coming wrath sets the ethical urgency of Colossians 3:5-17.
7: In these you too once walked, when you were living in them — the once/now schema (pote/nyn) is Paul's standard conversion narrative structure. The Colossians' Gentile past was marked by these vices; their present is defined by Christ's death and resurrection (Colossians 3:1-4). The narrative contrast motivates the ethical imperative.
8: But now you must put them all away: anger, wrath, malice, slander, and obscene talk from your mouth — the second vice list (orgē, thymos, kakia, blasphēmia, aischrologia) focuses on relational and verbal sins that disrupt community. The distinction between the two lists: Colossians 3:5 addresses vices primarily regarding God and self; Colossians 3:8 addresses vices primarily regarding others in community.
9: Do not lie to one another, seeing that you have put off the old self (ton palaion anthrōpon) with its practices — the old self/new self (palaios anthrōpos/kainos anthrōpos) contrast reflects the Adam-typology: the old self is the Adam-pattern of existence under sin and death (Romans 5:12-21); the new self is the Christ-pattern of existence in righteousness and life.
10: And have put on the new self, which is being renewed in knowledge after the image of its creator — the image of God (eikōn tou ktisantos) echoes Genesis 1:26-27: humanity created in the image of God, which image was damaged in the Fall and is now restored in the new creation. The knowledge (epignōsis) in which renewal occurs is the relational knowledge of God in Christ.
11: Here there is not Greek and Jew, circumcised and uncircumcised, barbarian, Scythian, slave, free; but Christ is all, and in all — the social categories being dissolved reflect the hierarchies of the Greco-Roman world: Greek/barbarian was the fundamental cultural distinction (Greeks were civilized; barbarians were those who could not speak Greek properly); Scythian was the extreme example of barbaric — the wild steppe peoples beyond the civilized world; slave/free was the fundamental social and legal distinction.
12: Put on then, as God's chosen ones (eklektoi), holy and beloved, compassionate hearts, kindness, humility, meekness, and patience — the virtue list (splagchna oiktirmou, chrēstotēs, tapeinophrosynē, praütēs, makrothymia) follows the vice lists of Colossians 3:5-9. The adoption of eklektoi (chosen, elect) as a descriptor for the Gentile church applies OT Israel's election vocabulary to the new covenant community.
13: Bearing with one another and, if one has a complaint against another, forgiving each other; as the Lord has forgiven you, so you also must forgive — the practice of communal forgiveness was formalized in the Lord's Prayer tradition (Matthew 6:12, 14-15) and the Matthew 18 community discipline procedure. The Lord's forgiveness as the model and ground of communal forgiveness reflects the covenant pattern.
14: And above all these put on love (agapēn), which binds everything together in perfect harmony (syndesmos tēs teleiotētos) — the syndesmos (binding ligament, bond) that holds the virtues together is love. The perfection (teleiotēs) that love produces reflects both Stoic and Platonic ideals of virtue-unity: the virtues form a unified whole. Paul's specific Christian content: the binding agent is love, not logos or reason.
15: And let the peace of Christ rule (brabeuetō) in your hearts, to which indeed you were called in one body — brabeuō (to umpire, to act as arbitrator, to rule as judge of a contest) was used for the official who awarded the prize in athletic games. The peace of Christ as the umpire/arbitrator of community disputes is a uniquely Pauline image for conflict resolution.
16: Let the word of Christ dwell in you richly, teaching and admonishing one another in all wisdom, singing psalms and hymns and spiritual songs — the threefold musical division (psalmos, hymnos, ōdē pneumatikē) reflects the actual diversity of early Christian song-practice. Psalms were the OT Psalter; hymns may have been christological pieces like Philippians 2:6-11 or Colossians 1:15-20; spiritual songs were spontaneous or prophetic compositions.
17: And whatever you do, in word or deed, do everything in the name of the Lord Jesus, giving thanks to God the Father through him — the all-encompassing scope (pan hoti ean poiēte — whatever you do) reflects the Stoic ideal of the logos governing all human activity. Paul christologically concretizes this: all activity is to be done in the name of the Lord Jesus.
18: Wives, submit to your husbands, as is fitting in the Lord — the household code (Haustafeln) in Colossians 3:18-4:1 follows a form found in Aristotle (Politics 1253b: the household's three pairs — husband/wife, father/children, master/slave) and developed by Hellenistic Jewish writers (Josephus, Philo) and Stoic moralists. Paul's version is Christianized by the repeated qualifier in the Lord/to the Lord.
19: Husbands, love your wives, and do not be harsh with them — the reciprocal obligation placed on husbands is significant: Greco-Roman household codes typically focused on the duties of the subordinate party. By commanding husbands to love and not be harsh, Paul inserts the mutual obligation that the conventional codes did not require of the dominant party.
20: Children, obey your parents in everything, for this pleases the Lord — the fifth commandment (Exodus 20:12; Deuteronomy 5:16) had a standard interpretation in both Jewish and Greco-Roman tradition. Philo wrote extensive commentary on it; Aristotle included parent/child relations in his household discussions. Paul's addition: this pleases the Lord — a distinctively theological motivation absent from secular household codes.
21: Fathers, do not provoke your children, lest they become discouraged — the reciprocal obligation to fathers: patria potestas (the father's legal authority over his household in Roman law) was nearly absolute, extending to the power of life and death. Paul limits this power by commanding fathers to avoid provoking children — a striking qualification of Roman paternal authority.
22: Bondservants (douloi), obey in everything those who are your earthly masters (kata sarka kyriois) — the slave instructions occupy the most space in the household code (Colossians 3:22-4:1), reflecting the social reality that many Colossian believers were slaves. The qualifier kata sarka (fleshly, earthly) distinguishes the human master from the divine kyrios (Lord) who is Christ.
23: Whatever you do, work heartily, as for the Lord and not for men — the working-for-the-Lord motivation transforms the slave's labor from coerced servitude into an act of worship. This was not a liberation theology in the modern sense — Paul did not call for the abolition of slavery — but it was a radical reframing of the slave's inner relationship to his work.
24: Knowing that from the Lord you will receive the inheritance as your reward — the inheritance (klēronomia) language applied to slaves was countercultural: slaves had no right to inheritance in Roman law. Paul tells the slave-believer that the divine inheritance awaits them — a cosmic reversal of their legal status.
25: For the wrongdoer will be paid back for the wrong he has done, and there is no partiality — the divine impartiality (prosōpolēmpsias — face-receiving, favoritism) is a standard Jewish and Jewish-Christian principle (Deuteronomy 10:17; Acts 10:34; Romans 2:11). The warning applies to all parties in the household code: the slave who wrongs will face divine judgment regardless of social status.
